Tsung Jen (Charles) Hsieh is a Master of Applied Science (MASc) student in the CENIT Research Group, where he focuses on the development and analysis of routing protocols within autonomous systems. He earned his Bachelor of Applied Science (BASc) degree with High Honours in Computer Engineering from the University of Toronto, with minor in Artificial Intelligence Engineering and certificate in Engineering Business. His emphasis is on the design and analysis of shortest-path routing protocols that are loop-free at every instance even in the midst of network changes.

Tsung Jen (Charles) Hsieh is a Master of Applied Science (MASc) student in the CENIT Research Group, where he focuses on the development and analysis of routing protocols within autonomous systems. He earned his Bachelor of Applied Science (BASc) degree with High Honours in Computer Engineering from the University of Toronto, with minor in Artificial Intelligence Engineering and certificate in Engineering Business. His emphasis is on the design and analysis of shortest-path routing protocols that are loop-free at every instance even in the midst of network changes.

J.J. Garcia-Luna-Aceves joined ECE in 2023 as Professor of ECE and Canada Excellence Research Chair at the University of Toronto. He is a Distinguished Professor Emeritus at the University of California, Santa Cruz (UCSC), and a Visiting Professor at the Computing Research Center of the National Polytechnic Institute in Mexico City, Mexico.
He received the B.S. degree in Electrical Engineering from the Universidad Iberoamericana, Mexico City, Mexico in 1977; and the M.S. and PhD degrees in Electrical Engineering from the University of Hawaii at Manoa, Honolulu, HI in 1980 and 1983, respectively.
Professor Garcia-Luna-Aceves has been a Principal Scientist at the Palo Alto Research Center (PARC), a Center Director at SRI International (SRI) in Menlo Park, California, a Visiting Professor at Sun Laboratories in Menlo Park, California, and a Principal of Protocol Design at Nokia in Mountain View, California. He has served as the Inaugural Chair of the ACM Special Interest Group on Multimedia and as General Chair of numerous conferences, including IEEE MASS 2023, IEEE ICNC 2016, ACM MSWIM 2015, ACM MobiCom 2008, IEEE SECON 2005, ACM Multimedia ’93 and ACM SIGCOMM ’88. He has also served as Program Chair of ACM MobiHoc 2002, ACM MobiCom 2000, IEEE MULTIMEDIA ’92, ACM SIGCOMM ’87 and ACM SIGCOMM ’86. He has been a keynote speaker at numerous conferences.
Professor Garcia-Luna-Aceves’s research focuses on computer communication and covers network architectures, communication protocols, distributed algorithms, digital infrastructures and performance analysis. He holds 76 US patents and has published more than 500 papers in journals, conferences and books. According to Google Scholar, his published work has received more than 44,000 citations and  his h-index is currently 105. He has directed more than 40 PhD theses and more than 40 M.S. theses. At UCSC, he directed the Computer Communication Research Group (CCRG) for 30 years, served as the Department Chair of the Computer Engineering Department and the Computer Science and Engineering Department, and also served as Campus Director of CITRIS and the Banatao Institute.

data: Joao is a PhD student in CENIT focusing on medium access control and routing in multi-hop multi-channel wireless ad hoc networks, considering directional communication enabled by multi-antenna devices and secure data transfer.

Prof. Jorg Liebeherr is a Professor in the Edward S. Rogers Sr. Department of
Electrical & Computer Engineering (ECE) at the University of Toronto.
For more than 30 years, he has conducted research in the area of
computer communication networks. His current research interests include
the characterization of network traffic of distributed machine learning
and low-cost sensor networks for environmental monitoring. He holds a
PhD degree in Computer Science from the Georgia Institute of Technology.
He is a Fellow of the IEEE.

Lindsay M. Montgomery is an Associate Professor of Anthropology and Indigenous Studies at the University of Toronto St. George campus.  She holds a B.A. (cum laude) in Human Rights & Anthropology from Columbia University and a PhD in Anthropology from Stanford University. Her research and teaching draws on feminist, collaborative, and anti-colonial approaches to documenting long-term land use practices and histories of inter-ethnic interaction in the American West.
Lucas has been a visiting Master student from the University of Applied Sciences Fulda in Germany.
Lucas has been a visiting Master student from the University of Applied Sciences Fulda in Germany.

Mahdi’s research focuses on decentralized wireless networks and next-generation multiple access protocols for future communication systems. His work studies how wireless users can share the channel efficiently without centralized scheduling, with the goal of improving effective throughput, reliability, and scalability in increasingly distributed and connected networks.
Michela Meo is a professor at the department of Electronics and Telecommunications, Politecnico di Torino universirty in Italy.

Dr Mohamed Chahine Ghanem is an Associate Professor and Cyber Security Research Lead at Keele University (UK), and a Visiting Associate Professor in Cyber Security and AI within the Cybersecurity Institute at the University of Liverpool. He also holds a Senior Advisor position in Cyber Resilience within the banking sector. Before joining academia, Dr Ghanem built a strong industry foundation with over 15 years of practice in senior positions across law enforcement and corporations, including as Global Cyber Security Auditing Director for Kroll LLC and Nationwide Bank. He holds an Engineering Degree in Computer Systems from EMP (Algeria), an MSc in Digital Forensics & IT Security, and a PhD in Cyber Security Engineering from City, University of London. Dr Ghanem is a Senior Fellow of the Higher Education Academy (SFHEA), holds an MA in Academic Practice, and has earned numerous respected certifications including CISSP, CPCI, multiple GIAC credentials, and the Digital Forensics ACE & XRY qualifications. He currently leads a research programme in applied AI for cyber security and supervises research students working on digital forensics, IoT security, offensive cyber security, and applied AI, with a record of publications in Q1 journals. Dr Ghanem is also a regular keynote speaker at leading international cyber security conferences. He is Editor-in-Chief of the Journal of Cyber Security and Risk Auditing (Q1), an Editor with ACM Digital Threats: Research and Practice, and a Guest Editor and Reviewer for leading cybersecurity venues including JCP, IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security (TIFS), and Elsevier Computers & Security.
data.research-interests#Research Interests:: Dr Ghanem's research sits at the intersection of artificial intelligence, cyber security, and next-generation digital infrastructure including but not limited to:Applied AI for Cyber Security · Intelligent & Intent-Aware Network Security · AI-Augmented Cyber Defence · Autonomous Cyber Operations · Offensive Security & Automated Testing · IoT & Edge–Cloud Security · Next-Generation Internet & Networking Protocols · Post-Quantum & Resilient Communications · Responsible & Governable AI

data: Mohammadreza is a PhD student in Electrical and Computer Engineering at the University of Toronto, supervised by Prof. J.J. Garcia-Luna-Aceves. His research focuses on security for routing protocols in computer networks, with an interest in applying cryptographic techniques to build verifiable and trustworthy network infrastructure.Prior to the University of Toronto, he completed his Master's in Information Technology Engineering at École de technologie supérieure (Université du Québec), where his work focused on robust federated learning and blockchain-based credential revocation. He holds a Bachelor's degree in Computer Engineering from Sharif University of Technology.

Dr. Morteza Moghaddassian joined CENIT in 2024 where he is currently a Senior Research Associate.
Prior to CENIT, Morteza has been a post-doctoral fellow in the Network Architecture Lab (NAL) at the Univerity of Toronto.
Morteza holds a PhD in Electrical and Computer engineering from the University of Toronto and his research interests
include network security for the current and the future Internet, communication protocols, network architectures,
cryptography, and the Internet of Things systems, protocols, and applications.
He is an ACM Professional member and a member of IEEE Computer and Communication Societies.

Philip is an Assistant Professor, Teaching Stream and Dean's Advisor for Black Inclusivity in the
Faculty of Applied Science and Engineering at the University of Toronto,
where he is affiliated with Institute for Studies in Transdisciplinary Engineering Education and Practice,
the Division of Engineering Science, and (by courtesy)
the Edward S. Rogers Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering.
His research interests are in the intersection of people and technology, and currently approaches this work
through the lens of innovation ecosystems.
Philip holds a Bachelors and Masters in Electrical Engineering from the University of Pennsylvania
and a PhD in Computer Engineering from the University of Virginia.
He is a Licensed Professional Engineer in Ontario.

Dr. Sardana Nikolaeva is a postdoctoral fellow with the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering of the University of Toronto. Her current work with the CENIT project focuses on the socio-technical dynamics of the digital divide, particularly among Indigenous peoples, and developing community research partnerships. Previously, she served as an inaugural postdoctoral fellow of Ziibiing Lab (Global Indigenous Politics Collaboratory) at the Department of Political Science. Sardana holds a PhD in Education from the University of Pittsburgh and a PhD in Cultural Anthropology from the University of Manitoba. Her research interests include Indigenous politics, extractivism, inequities in the Indigenous Arctic, epistemic violence and Indigenous research ethics.

Prof. Dr. Sebastian Rieger is a Professor of Multimedia Communication Networks at Fulda University of Applied Sciences, Germany, where he leads the MMNet research group in the Department of Applied Computer Science. He has held this professorship since 2012, following research and management positions at the Steinbuch Centre for Computing (SCC) at the Karlsruhe Institute of Technology (KIT) and at the Gesellschaft für wissenschaftliche Datenverarbeitung (GWDG) in Göttingen, where he worked on identity management, network and system management, and streaming media in wireless networks. He received his doctorate from the Institute for Information Systems at the University of Göttingen in 2007, with a dissertation on unified authentication in heterogeneous IT infrastructures for secure eScience environments.
His current research interests include network digital twins, resilient inter-domain routing, green intent-based service-aware routing, network services for local AI, and AI-augmented network automation and management. He teaches courses on computer networks and services as well as cloud computing at Fulda University of Applied Sciences, and his group's work is regularly presented at international networking conferences. He is a reviewer for IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management (TNSM) and IEEE/ACM Transactions on Networking (TNET).
Selected Recent Work:
Papers on Network Digital Twins, resilient BGP, and intent-based routing at IEEE/IFIP CNSM, IEEE/IFIP NOMS, IRTF NMRG and IFIP Networking
Public open source projects: DigSiNet / DigSiViz (network digital twin verification, visualization, and what-if analysis), learn-sdn-hub (virtual lab for programmable network education and experimentation)

Sheideh Homayon is a PhD candidate at the University of California, Santa Cruz, where her research focuses on wireless networks, medium access control (MAC) protocols, and network simulation. Her work centers on the design and evaluation of MAC-layer protocols through detailed simulation-based analysis. She holds a master's degree from the University of California, Santa Cruz, and a bachelor's degree in computer software engineering from Allameh Mohaddes Nouri University in Iran. Her research aims to advance the performance and efficiency of next-generation wireless communication systems.

Tailai Song received his Ph.D. in Electrical, Electronics and Communications Engineering under the supervision of Prof. Michela Meo and Prof. Marco Mellia from Politecnico di Torino, Italy, in 2026. He is a member of the SmartData@Polito Research Center and the Telecommunication Networks Group. He also received his B.Sc. in Automotive Engineering and M.Sc. in ICT for Smart Societies from Politecnico di Torino in 2020 and 2022, respectively. His research lies at the intersection of computer networks, communication systems, cybersecurity, and artificial intelligence. He has authored more than 15 papers published in established international conferences and journals.

data.join-cenit#Join CENIT!:
Thank you for your interest in joining our team!
We are always happy to hear from motivated students, postdocs, and researchers who are interested in seeing
if CENIT might be the right place for them.
Please contact team[@]cenit-uoft[dot]org to speak to us or apply for the available positions below.
data.position-details#Position Details:
We are seeking international and domestic PhD graduates who are interested in performing cutting-edge research in the areas of computer networks and security.
Successful candidates will focus on the design, implementations, and evaluation of communication protocols, network architectures,
and network security for the future Internet.
Successful candidates must obtained their PhD in the last 5 years in computer science/engineering, or in a related field.
Students who are in their final stage of their PhD studies are encouraged to apply.
data.funding#Funding: Funding will be available to successful applicants accoridng to their  admission pathways.
data.expression-of-interest#Expression of Int:
If you think CENIT is a right place for your academic adventures, we would like to hear from you.
We expect prospective candiadates to send us an email expressing their interest in conducting research at CENIT.
In your email, please include the followings:
A current CV
The name and email address of two academic references who have been engaged with you in a reseach project.
A two-page research statement describing:
Why you are interested in conducting research at CENIT, and,
Your past relevant experties in the CENIT's research areas.
We will ask you for more information regarding your interest if we require additional information.
You can learn more about our research by reviewing CENIT's  research thrusts  and
our  publications .
data.accepted-applicants#Accepted applicants:
Accepted applicants will receive an admisison offer from the 
Postdoctoral office of the School of the Graduate Studies  at the University of Toronto and must meet the office requirements for
postdoctoral fellowship at the University of Toronto.
Full-time Graduate Students
data.full-time-graduate-students#Full time Gr: CENIT is accepting applications for Fall (September) 2027.
data.what-we-look-for#What We Look For:
We are seeking highly talented and motivated international and domestic PhD and Master students that are interested in conducting cutting-edge research in the
areas of computer networks and security. Successful candidates will focus on the design, implementations, and evaluation of communication protocols, network architectures, and network security for the future Internet.
data.funding-1#Funding:
All accepted graduate students receive a guaranteed funding package as defined by the Electrical and Computer Engineering department at the University of Toronto.
You can review the latest updates on the Electrical and Computer engineering department's  funding packages 
before you apply.
data.expression-of-interest-1#Expression of I:
Before you submit your application to the Electrical and Computer Engineering department, you must email us and express your interest to study at CENIT.
We review your interest and get in touch with you. In your email, please include the followings:
A current CV
A cover letter stating your interests in CENIT's research directions and how you think CENIT can benefit you in your education and career pathways.
We will ask you for more information regarding your interest if we require additional information.
You can learn more about our research by reviewing CENIT's  research thrusts  and
our  publications .
data.final-application-submission#Final Appli:
Successful applicants who have thier interests approved by CENIT must submit their
 final application 
to the department of Electrical and Computer engineering at the University of Toronto.
Please be mindful of the  application deadlines 
and get in touch early on to avoid missing the deadlines.
Successful applicants must meet all the requirements set by the school of graduate studies and the department of Electrical and Computer engineering to receive an admission.
 Administrative Assistant (Term)
data.administrative-assistant-term---1-positi: We are hiring an administrative assistant to help with the efficient daily operation of CENIT.
data.your-responsibilites-include#Your Respon:
Reviewing and assessing requirements needed to meet project timelines
Coordinating meeting schedules, agendas, materials, action and follow-up items
Handling sensitive and/or confidential information
Maintaining senior staff calendars
Scheduling travel plans and requesting appropriate accommodations
Drafting presentation materials
Building and strengthening relationships with stakeholders and partners of strategic importance
Conceptualizing, organizing and executing event activities
data.essential-qualifications#Essential Quali:
Bachelor's Degree or acceptable combination of equivalent experience.
Minimum five years recent and related administrative experience in an autonomous, decision making position, preferably in a University environment.
Experience supporting faculty research, research centres and groups.
Experience drafting correspondence, preparing briefing packages, applying and working with policies and procedures and organizing and coordinating special events.
Demonstrated experience in liaising with senior administrators, responding to and handling matters of a complex, confidential and sensitive nature.
Experience with organizing complex meeting schedules.
Understanding of the University’s mission and intersection with the broader community.
Advanced computer skills in Microsoft Office (must be able to set up templates and macros in Word and Excel, set up Excel spreadsheets and graphics, and prepare sophisticated PowerPoint presentations), and scheduling software.
Demonstrated familiarity with Zoom, Microsoft SharePoint and Teams.
Demonstrated analytical and problem-solving skills.
Demonstrated superior writing and editing skills.
Superior communications skills, both written and verbal (e.g., drafting written correspondence, minutes, proposals).
Excellent organizational and time-management skills. Ability to set priorities, manage multiple tasks and projects and meet deadlines.
Proven track record of using good judgment in dealing with issues in a fast-paced, dynamic environment. Excellent interpersonal skills.
Ability to work collaboratively and positively in a team setting, working effectively with a wide range of people and personalities (members of the University and corporate communities, students, etc.).
Thoroughness and careful attention to detail. Tact, discretion, sensitivity to confidential matters.
data.how-to-apply-and-additional-information#:
Please see  the complete job advertisement 
on the University of Toronto website for more information and to apply for the job.
Applications must be submitted directly through the university's job posting.
 International Visiting Scholars 
We welcome visiting domestic and international graduate students for research visits. CENIT is also committed to collaborate internationally and across Canada with
researchers and faculties that find  CENIT's research thrusts  inline with their interests. Please contact us for more information.
 Internship at CENIT 
Students that are currently enrolled at a graduate or undergraduate program at the University of Toronto who have an interest to expand
their experties can conduct a time-limit internship at CENIT. Please contact us if you are interested.

data.ion-intent-oriented-networking#ION: Inte: We study networking technologies to allow end users and processes to state the intent of what service the Internet or a network infrastructure should provide for them with a high-level description, enable the network to automatically map user requirements to performance and policy criteria, and have the network deliver the best performance that satisfies user requirements taking into account network conditions.

data: We are developing the knowledge necessary for creating just and equitable digital infrastructures based on the results of the other three thrusts. To achieve this goal, JEDI is socio-economically aware and community-engaged.

data.mints-manifest-based-internet-transport-: We study a clean-slate redesign of the way in which remote processes of digital infrastructures communicate over the Internet and computer networks to support end-user applications and services. The proposed research focuses on eliminating the need for end-to-end connections to support reliable communication, making end-to-end reliable communication efficient for any type of bandwidth-delay product, enabling multipoint services without the need to adopt complex routing protocols, and enabling the Internet itself to serve as an intelligent and secure memory layer for all end-user applications and services.

data.swan-self-reliant-and-wise-ad-hoc-networ: We study the use of machine learning and use results from the other research thrusts to enable self-reliant networks that provide fair and efficient use of shared wireless bandwidth taking into account the connectivity and past history of interference caused by node transmissions. Our research aims to eliminate the current limitations of wireless networks based on such standards as WiFi and 5G.
